coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode thermochromic afterglow in phosphorus-doped carbon dot composites for visible light-activated information encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Xin
Zhu, Xiaodong
Tian, Zhen
Wang, Hui
Li, Hui
Yuan, Xi
coredata.dc:description: The phenomena of thermochromic afterglow have attracted significant attention in advanced information encryption and anticounterfeiting applications. However, it is still a challenge to realize visible light-activated thermochromic afterglow in a single material. Herein, we demonstrate thermochromic afterglow under visible light excitation in a novel carbon dot (CD)-based composite, utilizing a temperature-dependent dual-mode afterglow mechanism. The composite (PCD@BA) is synthesized by incorporating phosphorus-doped CDs (PCDs) into an inorganic rigid matrix via covalent bonding. Notably, phosphorus doping significantly enhances the photoluminescent properties and redshifts the excitation wavelength of the PCD@BA composite, enabling it to exhibit bright thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) when activated by white light. The thermochromic afterglow of the PCD@BA composite exhibits dynamic color transitions from deep blue to orange by tuning the proportion of the temperature-dependent room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and TADF. Consequently, the concepts of white light-excited high-resolution multilevel afterglow 2D code information encryption and thermochromic afterglow based anticounterfeiting labels were developed. This work offers exciting opportunities for utilizing CD-based materials with visible light-activated thermochromic afterglow in advanced information security fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Thermochromic afterglow
Visible light activated
Room temperature phosphorescence
Thermally activated delayed fluorescence

Synthesis of PCD@BA and BCD@BA composite
Structure characterization and mechanism studies of PCD@BA composite
Mechanism studies of thermochromic afterglow in PCD@BA composite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photoluminescence emission–excitation spectra of PCD@BA powder. (b) Corresponding images of the PCD@BA composite powder, taken before and after excitation using UV lamps (295 and 365 nm) and white light. (c) Excitation-dependent afterglow spectra of the PCD@BA composite. (d) Afterglow excitation spectrum of PCD@BA composite (at 535 nm). (e) Afterglow decay curves of the PCD@BA composite at 450 and 535 nm (under excited at 290 and 450 nm).

(a) Photoluminescence and afterglow spectra of the PCD@BA (290 nm excited). (b) Temperature-dependent afterglow spectra of PCD@BA (290 nm excited). (c) CIE coordinate diagram of the PCD@BA composite at different temperatures (290 nm excited). (d) Photoluminescence and afterglow spectra of the PCD@BA (450 nm excited). (e) Temperature-dependent afterglow spectra of PCD@BA (450 nm excited). (f) CIE coordinate diagram of the PCD@BA composite at different temperatures (450 nm excited).

(a) TEM image of PCD@BA. (b) SEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping images for B, C, and P of PCD@BA. (c) XRD spectra of the PCD@BA composite and annealed BA powders. (d) FTIR spectra of PCD@BA. High-resolution (e) C 1 s and (f) B 1 s XPS spectra of the PCD@BA.
Illustration of the photophysical processes in the PCD@BA composite under (a) 290 nm and (b) 450 nm excited.

(a) Schematic presentation of school emblem encrypted in a printed 2D code with thermochromic afterglow behaviors under visible light excitation, the PCD@BA aqueous solution was used as afterglow ink. (b) Schematic presentation of multilevel information encryption under UV/ white light excitation. (c) Thermochromic afterglow behaviors of the PCD@BA composite under UV/white light excitation.
Schematic illustration of the preparation processes of PCDs and PCD@BA composite.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode thermochromic afterglow in phosphorus-doped carbon dot composites for visible light-activated information encryption

Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Aviation University of Air Force, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Aviation University of Air Force, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The phenomena of thermochromic afterglow have attracted significant attention in advanced information encryption and anticounterfeiting applications. However, it is still a challenge to realize visible light-activated thermochromic afterglow in a single material. Herein, we demonstrate thermochromic afterglow under visible light excitation in a novel carbon dot (CD)-based composite, utilizing a temperature-dependent dual-mode afterglow mechanism. The composite (PCD@BA) is synthesized by incorporating phosphorus-doped CDs (PCDs) into an inorganic rigid matrix via covalent bonding. Notably, phosphorus doping significantly enhances the photoluminescent properties and redshifts the excitation wavelength of the PCD@BA composite, enabling it to exhibit bright thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) when activated by white light. The thermochromic afterglow of the PCD@BA composite exhibits dynamic color transitions from deep blue to orange by tuning the proportion of the temperature-dependent room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and TADF. Consequently, the concepts of white light-excited high-resolution multilevel afterglow 2D code information encryption and thermochromic afterglow based anticounterfeiting labels were developed. This work offers exciting opportunities for utilizing CD-based materials with visible light-activated thermochromic afterglow in advanced information security fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Long-lived afterglow emissions have aroused significant attention in the field of information encryption, sensors, bio-imaging, and illumination due to the ultra-long luminescence lifetime, high signal-to-noise ratio, and large Stokes shifts [1–9]. Based on the photoluminescent mechanism, the afterglow is typically classified into room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and delayed fluorescence (DF) [10,11]. Traditional afterglow materials are primarily composed of inorganic substances based on rare-earth elements or noble metals, which present potential environmental toxicity and incur high costs. Organic afterglow materials have also emerged in recent years, but their complex preparation methods and inadequate stability issues remain unresolved. In addition, conventional afterglow materials typically exhibit single-mode afterglow emission, either RTP or DF. Combining RTP and DF emissions within a single material can result in the phenomenon of thermochromic afterglow, which has great potential to enhance security in information encryption [12–15]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop novel afterglow materials that exhibit dual-mode afterglow emissions, possess excellent stability and biocompatibility, are cost-effective, and can be synthesized easily to facilitate advanced information encryption.

As an emerging type of 0 dimension (D) carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered considerable attention due to their low toxicity, low cost, ease of synthesis, and high photostability [16–21]. Some CD-based composites achieved high-efficiency afterglow by incorporating CDs in a rigid matrix (such as cyanuric acid, silica, zeolite, etc.) through covalent or hydrogen bonds [22–26]. This approach effectively suppresses non-radiative transitions and enhances the intersystem crossing (ISC) process between singlet and triplet states [27,28]. Heteroatom doping in CDs could also change the bandgap and narrow the energy gap between singlet and triplet states (ΔEST) [29,30]. By absorbing nearby thermal energy, the reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process is significantly enhanced, thereby promoting thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) in CD-based composite systems [31–34]. Recently, the phenomenon of the thermochromic afterglow has been realized in some single compound CD-based composites with the RTP-TADF dual mode afterglow, which has great potential in dynamic information encryption [35–39]. For instance, Zong et al. achieved RTP and TADF dual emission by confining CDs in a zeolite framework, and based on the thermochromic and time dependent color properties, the composite exhibits the afterglow color from blue to red [40]. However, it remains a significant challenge to achieve thermochromic afterglow in CD materials under visible-light excitation. In addition, the inkjet printing technology holds distinct advantages in advanced optical applications. To the best of our knowledge, the printable CD materials with thermochromic afterglow for information encryption have only been reported by Liu et al. under UV light excitation, which realized dynamic color changes from blue to yellow with the change of temperature [41]. Visible light (such as the white light emitted by a mobile phone flashlight) is safer for human health and more easily accessible in daily life compared to UV light [42,43]. Consequently, the development of visible light activated thermochromic afterglow in printable CDs-based materials is of significant scientific interest.

In this work, visible light excited thermochromic afterglow is realized in a novel CD-based composite through the dual-mode triplet emission mechanism of RTP and TADF. The PCD@BA composite was synthesized by incorporating phosphorus-doped carbon dots (PCDs) into a rigid matrix via covalent bonding during the thermal treatment of PCDs with boric acid (BA). P-doping changes the bandgap of CDs and enhances the high-efficiency TADF emission in the composite, which peaks at 535 nm with a lifetime of 193 ms under 450 nm light excitation (more than 6 s to the naked eye). Additionally, the composite exhibits a thermochromic afterglow achieved through dual-mode tuning of RTP and TADF under UV/visible light excitation, resulting in dynamic color transitions from deep blue to orange. Finally, we developed the concepts of white light-excited high-resolution multilevel afterglow 2D code information encryption using inkjet printing technology and thermochromic afterglow-based anticounterfeiting labels, thereby demonstrating the potential of CD-based materials for advanced optical applications.

m-phenylenediamine (with a purity of 99 %) and BA were obtained from Macklin (China), ethanol and phosphoric acid (PA) were obtained from Fuyu. All reagents were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the PCDs and BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PCDs and BCDs were synthesized via a solvothermal reaction following our previously reported method [44], and the reaction conditions were optimized based on the photoluminescent properties of the composites.

For the PCDs with phosphorus-doped, 0.4 g of m-phenylenediamine, 1.6 mL of phosphoric acid, and 25 mL of ethanol were selected as the precursor materials. The mixture was stirred until a transparent solution formed and then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave. After a heating treatment for 12 h (180 °C), the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min to remove the large particles. The supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm nylon membrane filters and then dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) against deionized water for 36 h. Finally, the dialyzed solution was dried at 100 °C to yield the PCD powder.

For the CDs without P element doping, only blue fluorescence emission could be observed in the CD aqueous solutions; therefore, we named them BCDs (blue-emissive CDs). The preparation of BCDs followed a similar preparation process as that for PCDs. Specifically, 0.4 g of m-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 25 mL of ethanol, and the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined high-pressure reactor, then heated for 12 h (180 °C). After centrifugation, filtration, dialysis, and oven drying, the BCD powder was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PCD@BA and BCD@BA composite
ce:section.ce:para: The composites of PCD@BA and BCD@BA were prepared using similar annealing treatment. 2 g of boric acid and 0.1 g of CDs were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 15 min until the precursor was fully dissolved. The resulting solution was then annealed in high-temperature oven for 3 h (210 °C). Finally, the obtained product was ground to obtain the PCD/BCD@BA composite powders.
The Annealed BA powders were obtained through heating treatment of BA, which was used to investigate the composition of the matrix material in the PCD@BA composite.
PCD@BA composite aqueous solution with a concentration of 0.05 g/ mL was used as encrypted security ink in the application of advanced information encryption using inkjet printing technology.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of PCDs and PCD@BA composite were obtained using a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and the corresponding elemental mapping images of PCD@BA composite materials were acquired using a JSM 5600LV scanning electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data of PCD@BA composite were collected using a Siemens D5005 diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of PCDs and PCD@BA composite were obtained with a KRATOS Axis Ultra spectrometer with an Al Ka excitation source, while Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of PCD@BA composite were recorded with a Perkin-Elmer spectrometer. A Shimadzu UV-3101PC spectrophotometer was used to measure all the ultraviolet (UV)-vis absorption spectra in this work, while the photoluminescence spectra were acquired employing a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrometer. The delay time for all the afterglow spectra presented in this work is set to 1 ms.

Based on the photoluminescent properties mentioned above, the origins of the afterglow in the PCD@BA composite were investigated by analyzing the photoluminescence spectra under 290 and 450 nm excitation. Fig. 2 a shows the steady-state photoluminescence and afterglow spectra of the PCD@BA (290 nm excited) at room temperature. The PCD@BA composite exhibits two fluorescence emissions at around 400–450 nm and 535 nm, along with two delayed emissions. The similar emission peak at 535 nm suggests that the green afterglow may result from delayed fluorescence, which is further verified by the temperature-dependent afterglow spectra (Fig. 2b). The intensity of blue emission in the temperature-dependent afterglow spectra decreases with increasing temperature from 77 to 340 K, thereby demonstrating the characteristic properties of RTP. In contrast, the green afterglow intensity continuously increases with the temperature rising from 77 to 320 K, indicating that it is attributed to TADF. Based on these temperature-dependent and dual-mode emissions, the PCD@BA composite exhibits thermochromic afterglow properties. As the temperature increased from 77 (low) to 340 K (high), the afterglow color of the PCD@BA composite gradually shifted from deep blue to pale yellow. The thermochromic afterglow color change in the temperature range of 77 to 340 K is illustrated on a Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE) coordinate diagram, as depicted in Fig. 2c. The shift of the thermochromic afterglow can be observed from coordinates (0.22, 0.23) to (0.35, 0.55) (Table S1). The thermochromic afterglow in the PCD@BA composite holds significant potential as an afterglow label for advanced anti-counterfeiting applications. Fig. 2d shows the steady-state photoluminescence and afterglow spectra of the PCD@BA excited by 450 nm at room temperature, the green fluorescence and afterglow peaks are centered at 535–540 nm. Temperature-dependent afterglow spectra excited by 450 nm (Fig. 2e) reveal that the green emission increases with rising temperature, further confirming their TADF properties. It is noteworthy that an orange emission peak centered at approximately 580 nm can be observed at low temperatures, which is indicative of the RTP nature. Based on the dual-mode afterglow emissions, the thermochromic afterglow shift is observed from coordinates (0.46, 0.48) to (0.30, 0.59) in the CIE coordinate diagram as the temperature increases from 77 to 340 K (Table S2). The thermochromic afterglow was also observed in the BCD@BA composite; however, the extent of color change is significantly weaker compared to that of the PCD@BA composite (Fig. S10). The steady-state and delayed photoluminescence spectra of the PCD@BA composite excited by 450 nm at 77 K were further characterized, and the emissions were centered at 538 and 576 nm (Fig. S11). The ΔEST between the singlet state (S) and the triplet state (T) was calculated to be 0.16 eV based on these emission peaks (the calculation process is provided in the Supporting Information). Smaller ΔEST (<0.3 eV) facilitates the rapid activation of the RISC process via thermal excitation [45], thereby promoting the generation of high-efficiency TADF emission under UV and visible light excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterization and mechanism studies of PCD@BA composite
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the photophysical mechanism of the PCD@BA composite, the structural characterization was examined in detail. Fig. 3 a shows the TEM image of the PCD@BA composite, where PCDs are uniformly dispersed within the matrix material. HR-TEM image of the PCD@BA composite reveals that the average particle size of the PCDs does not show an obvious change after annealing treatment (Fig. S12). The SEM images and corresponding EDS mapping of B, C, and P in the PCD@BA composite are shown in Fig. 3b, providing further evidence for the homogeneous distribution of PCDs within the matrix material. To investigate the composition of the matrix materials, the XRD patterns of the PCD@BA composite and annealed BA powders were measured and are presented in Fig. 3c. The similar characteristic peaks of B2O3 at 14.7°, 27.9°, 30.4°, and 40.2° were clearly observed in them [46,47]. The results demonstrated that BA was dehydrated and transformed into B2O3 during the annealing treatment. B2O3 is the main component of the matrix in the PCD@BA composite, forming a rigid framework within the composite system. Then, the structure of the PCD@BA composite was further characterized by FTIR and XPS spectroscopic analyses. As shown in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3d), the characteristic peaks at 1446, 1195, and 880 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of B-O, B-OH, and B-C, respectively [46,48]. The presence of the characteristic B-C stretching vibration peak in the FTIR spectra provides evidence that the PCDs are embedded within the B2O3 matrix through covalent bonding. Full XPS spectrum of the PCD@BA composite was shown in Fig. S13, and the HR-XPS spectra of C 1 s (Fig. 3e) could be fitted with four peaks at 288.7, 285.8, 284.8, and 283.8 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of CO, CN/CO, CC/CC, and C-B, respectively [49]. In Fig. 3f, the HR-XPS spectra of B 1 s demonstrated three peaks centered at 194.5, 193.3, and 192.1 eV belonging to B-O, B2O3, and BCO2, respectively [46]. The XPS spectra further confirm that the PCDs are incorporated into the matrix via covalent bonds, which is consistent with the FTIR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism studies of thermochromic afterglow in PCD@BA composite
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above discussion of the photophysical and structural characterization of the PCD@BA composite, possible mechanisms for the UV/visible light-activated thermochromic afterglow are proposed. After the annealing and dehydration process, BA is translated into B2O3, providing a rigid environment for the composite system. The PCDs are in-situ embedded within the matrix through covalent bonds. These structural features significantly enhance the ISC process and limit non-radiative transitions in the triplet states. In addition, the doping of P elements modifies the bandgap of the CDs through the strong electron-withdrawing effect, significantly enhancing the photoluminescent properties of the composite system. Under high energy light excitation, the afterglow emission in the PCD@BA originates from two triplet emission centers which peaking in the blue and green regions. The temperature-dependent afterglow spectra further demonstrate that the two-color afterglow corresponds to dual-mode emission (RTP and TADF). As shown in Fig. 4 a, triplet state emission occurs simultaneously at both emission centers under high-energy UV light excitation, such as 290 nm. The blue afterglow corresponds to RTP, where excitons in the singlet states undergo ISC process to the triplet states, followed by a radiative transition to the ground state (excitons: S0-S2-T2-S0) [30,44,49,50]. For the green afterglow emission, due to the narrow ΔEST some triplet excitons undergo reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) to return to the singlet state and followed by a radiative transition to the ground state, resulting in TADF emission (excitons: S0-S1-T1-S1-S0). The thermochromic afterglow origins from the temperature-dependent variation in emission intensities of the TADF and RTP processes: at lower temperatures, the non-radiative transitions of triplet excitons are significantly suppressed, leading to a gradual increase in RTP emission. As the temperature gradually increased, TADF emission intensified due to the RISC process. The interplay between these two emission processes gives rise to the observed thermochromic afterglow phenomenon.

Long lifetime TADF realized in PCD@BA composite under visible light excited, which is primarily due to the introduction of the phosphorus element. This modification induces heavy-atom effects and a strong electron-withdrawing effect in the PCDs, which results in the narrowing of the energy gap between the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO). Consequently, the energy required to excite electrons to higher states is significantly reduced, leading to a redshift in the excitation wavelength of the photoluminescence to the visible light region. Furthermore, for the PCD@BA composite, the triplet states are effectively stabilized by covalent bonding, enabling the material to exhibit visible light-excited afterglow. This phenomenon is consistent with previous findings in F and Cl doped CDs composites [29,30], where the stabilization of triplet states similarly facilitated efficient afterglow emission under visible light excitation. Fig. 4b shows the photophysical processes in PCD@BA composite under 450 nm excitation. Under lower temperatures, the orange RTP originates from the radiative transition of excitons in T1 (excitons: S0-S1-T1- S0). As the temperature increased, the non-radiative transition in T1 was enhanced, leading to the suppression of RTP emission. However, some excitons transition to the triplet states via the ISC process and then undergo the RISC process to return to the singlet state through the narrow ΔEST. The radiative transition of these excitons results in green TADF emission under visible light excitation (excitons: S0-S1-T1-S1-S0). Therefore, visible light-activated thermochromic afterglow is achieved in the PCD@BA composite through a dual-mode afterglow mechanism.

Visible light is safer and more convenient to acquire than UV light, making the development of visible light-excited inkjet printing for information encryption highly desirable. Inspired by the exceptional thermochromic afterglow and visible light-excited TADF properties, the CD@BA aqueous solution was employed as ink to develop advanced information encryption using inkjet printing technology. As shown in Fig. 5 a, a 2D code generated from a school emblem was printed on A4 paper using PCD@BA inks. The pattern displays a thermochromic afterglow, shifting from green to orange as the temperature decreases from 300 to 77 K under white light excitation, and the 2D code can be scanned by a mobile phone to retrieve the school emblem in the whole process. The high-resolution multilevel afterglow information encryption was achieved under UV/ visible light excitation through inkjet printing and modulation of water content. As shown in Fig. 5b, after printed on A4 paper, the 2D code is visible and can be scanned by a mobile phone to retrieve the information under UV light (365 nm) excitation, but it remains invisible under white light. The 2D code with cyan afterglow remains recognizable by a mobile phone even after the UV light is turned off. More importantly, a high-resolution 2D code with green afterglow emission emerges, and the encrypted school emblem can also be retrieved when the pattern is scanned by a mobile phone following white light irradiation. The encrypted information can be easily concealed: the afterglow emission in the 2D code is quenched by spraying water on the paper, due to the suppression of the radiative transition and the RISC process of triplet excitons. The afterglow emission in the 2D code can then be restored after the paper is annealed and the water evaporates. Based on these processes, UV/ visible light-excited inkjet printing of multilevel 2D code information encryption is achieved.

Furthermore, due to its significant thermochromic afterglow properties, the PCD@BA composite shows great potential as an afterglow label for commercial applications. As shown in Fig. 5c, after excitation by 295 nm light, the circular pattern based on PCD@BA powders exhibited a deep blue afterglow signal at 77 K. As the temperature increased from 77 to 340 K, the afterglow color transitioned from blue to green and then to yellow. Similarly, after excitation by white light from a mobile phone flashlight, the afterglow color shifted from orange to yellow and finally to green with the temperature increasing. These phenomena suggest that the PCD@BA composite has the potential for use in advanced optical anticounterfeiting, leveraging its thermochromic afterglow properties.

In summary, a novel CDs-based thermochromic afterglow material was synthesized by incorporating PCDs into a B2O3 matrix via covalent bonding during thermal treatment. The structure significantly suppresses the non-radiative transitions of triplet excitons and promotes the composite to exhibit dual-mode afterglow emission through RTP and TADF. P-doping changes the bandgap of CDs, resulting in a high-efficiency green TADF emission peaked at 535 nm with a lifetime of 196 ms under 450 nm excitation. Furthermore, the emission signal remained visible to the naked eye for up to 6 s following irradiation with a mobile phone flashlight. More importantly, the UV/visible light-excited thermochromic afterglow properties of the PCD@BA composite were realized through the varying intensities of RTP and TADF emissions at different temperatures. Consequently, we proposed the concept of high-resolution multilevel 2D code information encryption using inkjet printing technology and developed anticounterfeiting measures based on the PCD@BA composite under white light excitation. This work offers significant opportunities for utilizing visible light-activated thermochromic afterglow CD-based composites in advanced information security applications.

Xin Bao: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Xiaodong Zhu: Investigation, Data curation. Zhen Tian: Methodology, Formal analysis. Hui Wang: Software, Formal analysis. Hui Li: Software, Formal analysis. Xi Yuan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (grant numbers: YDZJ202301ZYTS254, 20230508058RC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.137331.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in the design of afterglow materials: mechanisms, structural regulation strategies and applications
Chem. Soc. Rev.
